title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Wimbourne and Claddock discuss the inheritance implications of a letter regarding the Crickenshaw family. Wimbourne explains that if a boy is proven to be Edmund Crickenshaw's son, born after marriage, he would inherit a portion of a trust from Jotham Crickenshaw and the Lutherdale manor. Claddock notes that Harold and Alfred Crickenshaw might be unsettled by this, as it could reduce their inheritance. While Claddock suggests they might have motives due to financial instability, Wimbourne argues that Harold would not benefit from killing his sister-in-law, Ruth.

Inspector Claddock and Weatherall later visit Harold Crickenshaw at his lavishly decorated city office to investigate his whereabouts on Friday, December 20th. Harold, who appears wealthy despite being near insolvency, initially reacts with anger and requests a lawyer. However, his secretary, Alice, provides a schedule for that day. Harold claims that after a morning meeting and lunch with Lord Fosseville, he returned to the office at 3 pm to dictate letters, visited Sotheby's Auction House, stopped at a shop called Russell on Jermyn Street for tea, spent thirty minutes at the New Theatre, and attended a 7:30 pm dinner at the Cartier Club before returning home to 43 Cardigan Gardens around 11:30 pm. 

Harold notes his wife is in the south of France and he drives a Hobgoblin car only on weekends, renting a garage at the Cadogan Garden Stables. After the meeting, Claddock theorizes that Harold might have used the 4:33 train to push the victim off, returned to London for dinner, and later drove to Larchfield Manor to move the body into a coffin. Claddock intends to verify Harold's presence at the auction house and the pub, and to investigate the stables.
